title: Chaussons aux pommes
subtitle: French apple turnovers in flaky puff pastry
excerpt: The classic French baker's apple turnover: an oval of all-butter puff pastry folded over a thick apple compote filling, brushed with egg wash, and scored with a curved pattern before baking. Best warm.
sourceNotes: Chaussons aux pommes are documented in French baking literature of the nineteenth century. The name (meaning 'slippers') refers to the turnover shape. Urbain Dubois documents similar apple-filled puff pastry preparations in La Cuisine Classique (1856). The method here follows the traditional French boulangerie approach: a thick, jammy apple compote with cinnamon and lemon, enclosed in all-butter puff pastry.

content.text: The filling for a chausson aux pommes is not a raw apple slice but a thick, cooked apple compote that holds together without leaking through the pastry. It should be almost paste-like, cooked down until no water remains. An uncooked or watery filling will blow out through the pastry seams in the oven.

attrs.prepNote: beaten with 1 tbsp milk for egg wash

content.text: Put the chopped apple, sugar, cinnamon, lemon zest, and butter in a saucepan over medium heat. Cook for 15 to 20 minutes, stirring regularly, until the apple is completely soft and the mixture has dried out to a thick, jammy paste. All visible liquid must be driven off. Spread on a plate to cool completely. Do not fill the pastry with warm compote or the butter in the puff pastry will begin to melt during assembly.

On a lightly floured surface, roll the pastry to 4 mm thick. Cut into 8 ovals roughly 14 × 10 cm using a sharp knife or by pressing a suitable template. Place a heaped tablespoon of the cold apple compote in the centre of each oval, leaving a clear 1.5 cm border. Brush the border with

. Fold the pastry over to form a half-moon shape and press the edges together firmly. Press the sealed edge with a fork or crimp between finger and thumb. Place on lined baking trays and refrigerate for 30 minutes.

Preheat the oven to 210°C conventional. Brush the tops of the chilled chaussons with the egg wash. Using the back of a knife, score curved parallel lines from the sealed edge to the rounded top in a feather pattern, press lightly, do not cut through the pastry. Bake for 25 to 30 minutes until a deep golden brown and the pastry is well risen and visibly layered. The base should be crisp, not soft. Cool on a rack for 10 minutes before serving.
